chunk_summary: Direct feeding observation is crucial for addressing poor weight gain in infants under 6 months, focusing on milk transfer issues, hydration, latch, and supplementation while protecting breastfeeding.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of direct feeding observation in infants with poor weight gain.
Identify signs of inadequate milk transfer and hydration in infants under 6 months.
Learn safe supplementation practices while supporting breastfeeding.
Recognize red flags requiring escalation or referral in faltering growth cases.
key_facts: Poor weight gain in infants under 6 months is often due to milk-transfer issues, not picky eating.
Weight loss exceeding 10% of birthweight or signs of dehydration requires urgent action.
Effective latch and rhythmic suck-swallow-breathe coordination are critical for milk transfer.
Supplementation should prioritize expressed breast milk or formula while maintaining breastfeeding frequency.
Red flags include tachypnea, sweating with feeds, hepatomegaly, recurrent vomiting, chronic diarrhea, or developmental concerns.
reference_quote: Recognition and management of faltering growth in children (NICE NG75) emphasizes direct feeding observation and safe supplementation decisions to address poor weight gain in infants under 6 months.
document_key_topics: Feeding difficulties in infants under 6 months
Escalation and referral for persistent faltering growth
